title: Midterm 1
text:
The questions on the in-class portion of the midterm on probability and the binomial test will be drawn from these questions. Please try each question and compare your solutions with your classmates.



Suppose a pair of fair six-sided dice is rolled once.
(a) List the sample space of all possible sums that can result from rolling the pair of dice once.
(b) How many ways can the sum of 7 be obtained when a pair of dice is rolled once?
(c) What is the probability of obtaining a sum of 7 if a fair pair of dice is rolled once?
(d) What is the probability of obtaining a sum of 7 or 11 if a fair pair of dice is rolled once?




A university health center is evaluating a screening test for a rare viral infection among students. 4% of students have the infection. If a student has the infection, the test is positive 95% of the time. If a student does not have the infection, the test is negative 90% of the time.
Let:

\(D\) = student has the infection
\(T^+\) = positive test result

(a) What is the probability that a randomly selected student tests positive?
(b) If a student tests positive, what is the probability that they actually have the infection?
(c) A student tests negative. What is the probability that they truly do not have the infection?
(d) Suppose 1,000 students are tested. How many students would you expect to test positive? Of those who test positive, how many would you expect to actually have the infection?
(e) A student says: “Since the test is 95% accurate, if I test positive there’s a 95% chance I’m infected.” Explain clearly why this statement is incorrect. Your explanation should reference the base rate (prevalence) of the disease.




For each of the four research questions (A–D) in the box below, be prepared to answer any of the follow-up questions 1–15.
Note that an observed value of \(x\) for each question will be provided on the in-class exam for you to analyze and make a conclusion from based on what we expected based on an appropriate plot of \(x\) vs. \(P(X = x)\).






NoteResearch questions A–D



A. ChatGPT estimates that 47% of adults in the US have more than one pet. Is the percentage of STAT 109 students who own multiple pets different than this figure? To find out, a statistics teacher plans to ask 40 students if they own more than one pet. The number of these students who do own more than one pet will be provided on the in-class midterm.
B. Is the percentage of college students with colorful dyed hair less than 15%? To find out, a statistics teacher plans to watch 20 students enter BSS and record \(x\) = number of them who have (in her opinion) colorful dyed hair. This observed value of \(x\) will be provided on the in-class midterm.
C. Is a pair of six-sided dice Rosanna purchased on eBay more likely to roll either a 7 or an 11 than a fair pair of dice? Suppose Rosanna is going to roll her pair of dice 35 times and record \(x\), the number of times she gets the pair to sum to either 7 or 11. Her observed value of \(x\) will be provided on the in-class midterm.
D. Is the probability of a correct forecast of rain in Arcata more than 0.9? Suppose data from 14 days will be collected and \(x\) = “the number of times rain was correctly forecast” counted. This observed value of \(x\) will be provided on the in-class midterm.




1. State what each of the 4 conditions of the Binomial Random Process represents in the context of the research question.
2. Describe in a sentence what \(p\) represents in this context.
3. State the null and alternative hypotheses using the symbol \(p\) for the research question.
4. Let \(X\) be the number of successes in \(n\) trials. On the take-home exam, you created a graph of the probability of \(X\) assuming the null hypothesis is true, for values of \(X\) between 0 and \(n\).






Important



Note: this graph will be provided to you in-class during the exam.


Suppose that when data was collected, \(x\) out of \(n\) trials was observed. Add this data to the graph and decide whether to reject or fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Circle one: Reject the null hypothesis  Fail to reject the null hypothesis
5. Based on your answer in part 4, write a one-sentence description of your conclusion based on the data about whether or not the null hypothesis is true.
6. Is a sample size of \(n\) large enough to use the Normal Approximation? Show your work in checking the technical condition for using the Normal Approximation to the Binomial.
7. Assume the sample size \(n\) is large enough for the Normal Approximation to be valid. Find the mean and standard deviation of \(X\), the number of successes out of \(n\) trials assuming that the null hypothesis is true. Then use the Empirical Rule and the Normal Approximation to draw the distribution of \(X\), the number of successes in \(n\) trials assuming the null hypothesis is true. Calculate and clearly label the bounds of the rejection region that excludes the middle 95% of the distribution.
8. Suppose that when data was collected, \(x\) out of \(n\) trials was observed. Add this data to the graph in 7 and decide whether to reject or fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Circle one: Reject the null hypothesis  Fail to reject the null hypothesis
9. Based on your answer in part 8, write a one-sentence description of your conclusion based on the data about whether or not the null hypothesis is true.
10. Let \(X\) be the number of successes in \(n\) trials. On the take-home exam, you created a graph of the probability of \(X\) assuming the null hypothesis is true, for values of \(X\) between 0 and \(n\).






Important



Note: this graph will be provided to you in-class during the exam.


Suppose that when data was collected, \(x\) out of \(n\) trials was observed. Add this data to the graph and shade the area that represents the \(p\)-value for the alternative hypothesis.
11. Write an expression using the pdf \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) of the Binomial random variable to compute the \(p\)-value for this data and research question with values for \(n\), \(p\), and \(x\) plugged in. Note: you don’t need to evaluate this expression, just write it out.
12. Suppose the \(p\)-value for this data and research question is 0.0001. What do you conclude about the null hypothesis based on the size of this \(p\)-value?
Circle one: Reject the null hypothesis  Fail to reject the null hypothesis
13. Suppose the \(p\)-value for this data and research question is 0.87. What do you conclude about the null hypothesis based on the size of this \(p\)-value?
Circle one: Reject the null hypothesis  Fail to reject the null hypothesis
14. Suppose the \(p\)-value for this data and research question is 0.0001. Write a one-sentence interpretation of this \(p\)-value in the context of the data observed and the null hypothesis. 15. Suppose the \(p\)-value for this data and research question is 0.87. Write a one-sentence interpretation of this \(p\)-value in the context of the data observed and the null hypothesis.

title: Midterm 2
text:
Midterm 2 will have two part style similar Midterm 1: a take-home computing with R component and an in-class component built from research questions and provided R output. All in-class questions will be drawn from the practice problems provided here.
Midterm 2 covers material after Midterm 1 through many-group / many-category inference (e.g. one-way ANOVA for several means; chi-squared for tables with several categories) and includes sampling, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, one variable summaries/plots, two-variable summaries/plots , and study design.
Any resource may be used on the take-home component while you will be provided with a “methods table” during the in-class component. You may also use a scientific calculator during the in-class exam.



Please fill in the document provided in Canvas using the dataset there.

Univariate plots (tidyverse / ggplot2). For each numerical variable you analyze, include at least one appropriate plot (e.g. histogram or dot plot). For each categorical variable you analyze, include an appropriate plot (e.g. bar chart of counts).
Bivariate plots. For each pair of variables the take-home prompt tells you to relate, include one appropriate plot (e.g. scatterplot for two numerical variables; side-by-side boxplots or faceted histograms for numerical vs binary/categorical; stacked or grouped bar chart for two categorical variables).
Univariate descriptive statistics. For numerical variables: \(n\), mean, SD, median, IQR (or what the prompt asks). For categorical variables: counts and proportions (overall or as directed).
Bivariate descriptive statistics. For example: grouped means/SDs, two-way table of counts, joint or row proportions—whatever matches the variable types and the prompt.
Use t.test or prop.test to obtain a p-value or confidence interval as appropriate for the number and type of variables in the research question.




For each numbered scenario below you will see:

A short study description
A research question.
A fixed checklist of questions you should be prepared to answer.
Provided R code and output for that scenario.

Fixed checklist (repeat for every scenario).

Parameter. What population quantity is the question mainly about? Define it in words and symbols (e.g. use \(\mu\), \(p\), \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\) as appropriate and say what they represent in context in a sentence).
Variables. How many variables, and what are their types (numerical or categorical)?
Purpose. Is a confidence interval, a hypothesis test, or descriptive statistics and plots only most appropriate for the stated research question and design? Give one sentence why.
Descriptive Statistics: name one plot and one numerical summary you use to describe the variable(s) in the dataset that will be used to answer the research question.
By hand, be prepared to sketch a histogram, boxplot, bar plot or 100% stacked bar plot from a small dataset. Also be prepared to compute the mean, median, IQR, range, 5-number summary and proportions by row or column by hand from a small dataset.
If a test: state \(H_0\) and \(H_a\) (symbols + one sentence each in context). List conditions you would check for the procedure you named and say whether they are met for the provided sample (with reasons).
If a CI only: state the parameter the interval estimates and list conditions for the interval method you named; say whether they are met.
After output is provided: interpret the most appropriate printed statistic or plot and, if appropriate, a confidence interval, or p-value in context. Write a one-sentence conclusion. When appropriate, also name study design and sampling method (e.g. RCT with random assignment and convenience sample) and use these to whether causal language is appropriate and whether the results from the study generalize to a larger population.




Study. A set of \(n = 42\) coordinates were randomly selected from a grid of coordinates in a forest. The closest Douglas-fir seedling to each of these coordinates had it’s stem diameter (mm) measured
Research Question With high confidence, what is the mean stem diameter (mm) of all Douglas-fir seedings in this forest?


# A tibble: 10 × 1
   stem_mm
     <dbl>
 1     6.3
 2     7.1
 3     7.8
 4     8.1
 5     6.5
 6     7.2
 7     7.7
 8     5.3
 9     6.3
10     7  


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  `mean(stem_mm)` `sd(stem_mm)` `n()`
            <dbl>         <dbl> <int>
1            6.84          1.14    42










    One Sample t-test

data:  A$stem_mm
t = 38.747, df = 41, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 6.479424 7.192005
sample estimates:
mean of x 
 6.835714

Research question. Is there a difference in mean systolic blood pressure between adults who are given a daily walking coach app vs. who are given a pamphlet?

Study. A set of \(n = 42\) coordinates were randomly selected from a grid of coordinates in a forest. The closest Douglas-fir seedling to each of these coordinates had it’s stem diameter (mm) measured
Research Question With high confidence, what is the mean stem diameter (mm) of all Douglas-fir seedings in this forest?

title: Course Syllabus
text: Welcome to STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics!

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 1

Monday
Holiday - No class


Wednesday
Lecture 1
Introduction — instructor, classmates, course, and what statistics is

Meet the teacher and your classmates.
Describe what STAT 109 covers and how lecture, lab, quizzes, and projects fit together.
Explain informally what statistics is and give examples of questions it can help answer.
Describe five main steps in the statistical process



Friday
Lecture 2
Probability as data vs theory

Compute counts, proportions, and percentages for an event across repeated trials.
Use symbols \(x\), \(n\), \(\hat{p}\), and \(P(\text{event})\) for data vs theoretical probability.
Distinguish observed outcomes from theoretical probability in a random process.
State three rules that probabilities must satisfy.
Define probability and connect it to long-run relative frequency.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 2

Monday
Lecture 3
Probability calculations the long way — sample spaces and events

Define and identify sample space, outcome, and event for a trial.
Use notation \(P(A)\) for the probability of an event.
State and use the formula for \(P(A)\) when outcomes in the sample space are equally likely.



Wednesday
Lecture 4
Random babies — probability by careful counting

List the sample space for randomly returning four babies to four mothers.
Count outcomes for events such as “all correct” or “exactly \(k\) correct.”
Compute probabilities when each pairing in the sample space is equally likely.
Compare exact probabilities to a simulation (e.g. Random Babies applet).



Friday
Lecture 5
Addition rule for probability

State the addition rule for any two events \(A\) and \(B\).
Use the addition rule to compute \(P(A \text{ or } B)\), including overlapping events.
Apply special cases: complement rule and non-overlapping events.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 3

Monday
Lecture 6
Conditional probability and multiplication rule

State the definition of conditional probability and compute \(P(A \mid B)\).
Use the multiplication rule to compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Decide whether two events are independent using equivalent criteria.



Wednesday
Lecture 7
Law of total probability and Bayes’ rule

State the law of total probability for a partition of the sample space.
State Bayes’ rule and relate the pieces to conditional and marginal probabilities.
Use tree diagrams to organize two-stage problems and find \(P(B)\) and \(P(A \mid B)\).



Friday
Lecture 8
Practice — Lectures 6–7 (diagnostic testing and Bayes)

Compute and interpret conditional probabilities from counts (e.g. sensitivity, false positive rate).
Assess independence in context and use the law of total probability for \(P(B)\).
Build tree diagrams and apply Bayes’ rule to find inverse probabilities such as \(P(C \mid T^+)\).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 4

Monday
Lecture 9
Permutations and combinations

Distinguish settings where order matters (permutations) from where it does not (combinations).
Use \(_nP_r\) and \(_nC_r\) (or \(\binom{n}{r}\)) to solve counting problems.
Relate factorials to the permutation and combination formulas.



Wednesday
Lecture 10
From coin tosses to the binomial random process

Define a discrete random variable and its probability distribution (pdf).
Define a binomial random variable and a binomial random process.
Match notation (\(n\), \(x\), \(p\)) and the four conditions for a binomial process to a coin-toss setting.
Express \(P(X = x)\) using the binomial pdf.



Friday
Lecture 11
Practice — binomial random process

Decide whether a scenario satisfies the four binomial conditions and justify your answer.
Identify \(n\), \(p\), and the random variable \(X\) for a binomial count.
Write and evaluate probabilities using the binomial pdf; use mean \(\mu = np\) and SD \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\) as needed.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 5

Monday
Lecture 12
Introduction to the binomial test (ESP example)

Translate a research question about a proportion into \(H_0\) and \(H_a\).
Check the four BRP conditions for data from a fixed number of binary trials.
Connect the ESP activity to the structure of Project 1.



Wednesday
Lecture 13
ESP worksheet — binomial test in practice

State hypotheses and verify the BRP conditions for your own ESP test data.
Identify \(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\) and record your observed count \(x\).
Obtain a p-value from the sample R notebook and share results as directed.



Friday
Lecture 14
Binomial test — steps and worked examples

Carry out the full workflow: question, parameter, hypotheses, \(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\), BRP check, null distribution plot, observed \(x\), shading by \(H_a\), p-value, and conclusion in context.
Handle one-sided and two-sided alternatives correctly when shading and interpreting the p-value.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 6

Monday
Lecture 15
Mean and standard deviation of a binomial random variable

Use \(\mu = np\) and \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\) for \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n,p)\).
Interpret the mean as the center and the SD as typical deviation of \(X\).
Identify \(n\) and \(p\) in applied settings and compute \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).



Wednesday
Lecture 16
Normal approximation to the binomial; one-proportion test by hand

Use the CLT to approximate a binomial distribution by a normal curve with the same \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).
Check conditions such as \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\) when appropriate.
State the Empirical Rule for normal distributions.
Sketch a rejection region under a normal curve for a one-proportion test and relate the shaded area to the p-value (approximate).



Friday
Lecture 17
Type I and Type II errors; power

Define Type I and Type II errors and describe each in context.
Define power as \(1 - P(\text{Type II error})\) and relate it to detecting a false \(H_0\).
Explain how significance level \(\alpha\) and sample size help balance error rates and power.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 7

Monday
Lecture 18
Sampling distributions and estimation

Define the sampling distribution of a statistic.
Describe confidence level, parameter, and the endpoints of a confidence interval in context.
Compute and interpret a confidence interval for a population proportion and for a population mean (methods from lecture).



Wednesday
Review
Review before Midterm 1

Consolidate probability rules, counting, binomial models, and binomial tests.
Bring questions about practice problems and course structure for the exam.



Friday
Midterm 1
Midterm 1 (in lecture)

Demonstrate understanding of material covered through the review session (see course schedule and practice materials).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 8

Monday
Lecture 19
Confidence intervals and the importance of sampling

Discuss bias and its impact given how a sample was collected.
Identify ways to reduce bias and increase precision.
Identify a sampling frame and carry out simple random sampling.
Classify methods as convenience, SRS, cluster, systematic, stratified, or multi-stage.



Wednesday
Lecture 20
Sampling methods in practice

Identify a sampling frame for a stated population.
Carry out simple random sampling using a numbered list and random draws.
Classify sampling designs and discuss bias, generalizability, and how to improve the design.



Friday
Lecture 21
Target & Rubberband Analogy

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 9

Monday
Lecture 22
Normal distribution and standardization

Compute a z-score from an observation and the mean and standard deviation of a reference distribution.
Interpret a z-score in context and use z-scores for standardized comparisons between groups or to a reference.
Find probabilities (areas) for the standard normal distribution and describe them as proportions of values below (or above) a cutoff.



Wednesday
Lecture 23
Organizing the statistics toolbox — z-test for a proportion, variable types, CI vs. testing

Compute a standardized test statistic using the normal approximation to the binomial and connect it to a one-proportion test (often called a z-test); in R, use prop.test() and read off a confidence interval as well.
Organize analyses by type of variable(s) and by purpose (e.g. description vs. inference).
Relate descriptive tools to inferential choices for one categorical variable (e.g. proportion, confidence interval vs. hypothesis test).



Friday
Lecture 24
One numerical variable — center, graphs, and the one-sample t-test

Compute mean and median by hand and with the tidyverse (pipe to summarize()).
Graph one numerical variable with ggplot2 using a histogram and a dot plot.
State the one-sample t-statistic and use t.test() for a one-sample t-test and confidence interval for the population mean.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 10

Monday
Lecture 25
One numerical outcome across two groups — spread, boxplots, and two-sample t

Compute range, quartiles, IQR, and standard deviation by hand and compare two small datasets.
Build and interpret boxplots as a picture of the five-number summary.
Describe the Welch two-sample t-test (independent groups, unequal variances), its t-statistic, degrees of freedom, hypotheses, and conditions; run t.test(y ~ group) in R.
Use group_by() with summarize() for descriptives and ggplot2 (fill or color) to compare groups with histograms, dot plots, or boxplots.



Wednesday
Lecture 26
Two numerical variables — scatterplots and describing relationships

Make a scatterplot in ggplot2 for two numerical variables (aes(x, y) and geom_point()).
Describe an association using form (e.g. linear vs curved), direction (positive, negative, or none), and strength (strong, moderate, weak, or none).



Friday
Lecture 27
Two categorical variables — contingency tables, row/column/total proportions, stacked bar charts

Build a two-way table of counts and compute joint, row, and column proportions (know which denominator you use).
Draw stacked bar charts for counts and 100% stacked bars (position = "fill") to show proportions within bars.
Implement tables and plots with count(), group_by(), mutate(), and pivot_wider().

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 11

Monday
Lecture 28
Inference for two-group comparisons — causal questions and study design

Compare randomized controlled trials and observational designs when the goal is a causal claim about a treatment or exposure.
Explain how participants end up in comparison groups in an RCT versus an observational study.
State what a p-value does and does not address for a difference between two groups.



Wednesday
Lecture 29
Confounding

Define a confounder in words and recognize plausible confounders in a stated observational comparison.
Explain how a confounder can create or distort an association between an explanatory variable and a response.
Describe subclassification / stratification when a confounder is measured.



Friday
Lecture 30
Study design, p-values, and two-group inference (Welch two-sample t-test, proportions, RCT examples)

Use the Methods table (Lecture 30) to pick t.test() for two independent numerical groups or prop.test() / chisq.test() for binary comparisons, and name key conditions (e.g. independence, expected counts).
Interpret Welch two-sample t-test output (hypotheses, p-value, confidence interval) in context.
Connect random assignment in an RCT to reduced risk of confounding for treatment–outcome comparisons.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 12

Monday
Lecture 31
Matched pairs — differences, plots, paired t-test, and McNemar for paired binary data

Identify a matched pairs design and compute within-pair differences (or use a paired test) when the same units contribute two measurements.
Summarize paired numerical data with plots (e.g. boxplot of differences) and run a paired t-test (or one-sample test on differences).
For two binary outcomes on the same units, build the right table and run McNemar’s test when appropriate.



Wednesday
Lecture 32
One-way ANOVA — comparing means across more than two groups

Recognize when the outcome is numerical and the explanatory factor has more than two levels, so a one-way ANOVA is the natural extension of a two-sample t-test.
State the omnibus null hypothesis for equal population means across groups.



Friday
Lecture 33
Chi-square tests — independence and goodness of fit

Build contingency tables of counts for two categorical variables and use chisq.test() as appropriate.
Check expected counts (and know when simulate.p.value = TRUE is needed).
Relate chi-square tests to the “by chance” explanation for a table.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 13

Monday
Lecture 34
Work session — Project 4 (simulation, notebook, and writeup)

Apply study-design ideas (RCT vs observational, confounding, DAG) to your assigned two-group question.
Produce a reproducible simulated dataset (set.seed) and a notebook with descriptives, plots, and the correct test/CI from the Methods table.
Draft or refine the one-page summary for a classmate (plan + sample results + link to runnable code).



Wednesday
Review
Review before Midterm 2

Consolidate two-group inference, study design, confounding, and the Methods table.
Bring questions about practice problems, Project 4, and the exam format.



Friday
Midterm 2
Midterm 2 (in lecture)

Demonstrate understanding of material covered through the Week 13 review (see course schedule and practice materials).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 14

Monday
Lecture 35
Correlation and simple linear regression

Interpret a correlation coefficient (strength and direction) for two numerical variables.
Fit and interpret a simple linear regression line as a model for how the mean of \(Y\) changes with \(x\).
Use scatterplots and diagnostic thinking about linear vs curved form.



Wednesday
Lecture 36
Linear regression inference — confidence and prediction intervals

Interpret confidence intervals for the slope and for the mean response at a chosen value of \(x\).
Interpret prediction intervals for an individual future response and explain why they are wider than mean-response intervals.
Connect residual assumptions to validity of interval-based inference.



Friday
Lecture 37
Logistic regression — modeling a binary outcome

Recognize when the response is binary and a logistic (log-odds) model is appropriate instead of ordinary least squares regression.
Interpret fitted probabilities or odds ratios from simple logistic output in context (at the level taught in this course).
Connect logistic regression to earlier ideas about proportions and two-group comparisons.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 15

Monday
Lecture 38
Ethics and statistical reasoning review

Use a truth-first lens to connect design, assumptions, missingness, and communication.
Evaluate ethical pitfalls in generalization, causal language, reporting, and model validity.
Practice responsible statistical wording and evidence-based conclusions.



Wednesday
Review
Project 5 work day

Finalize team predictor choice, measurements, and data collection.
Begin/finish analysis workflow and draft the individual one-page summary.
Confirm team-member contribution statements.



Friday
Review
Project portfolio review (Projects 1-5)

Synthesize methods and conclusions across all five projects.
Identify strongest evidence statements and common interpretation pitfalls.
Prepare final revisions for Project 4 and Project 5 submissions.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Overview
This course includes several types of assessments designed to help you learn and to demonstrate your understanding of statistical concepts and methods.








Component
Weight
Description




Daily Participation
5%
Warm-up problems in lecture (Mondays, Wednesdays, Fridays)


Weekly quizzes
30%
Quizzes on Thursdays at the beginning of lab


Weekly labs
15%

title: Course Syllabus
text:
R Software Downloads
STAT 109 uses R for statistical computing. You can use R in several ways:

Base R
Download R: R Project Website
R is free and open-source statistical software. The base R installation includes the core R language and basic packages.

Windows: Download the installer from CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network)
Mac: Download the macOS installer from CRAN
Linux: Install via your distribution’s package manager or from CRAN



RStudio
Download RStudio: RStudio Desktop
RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R that provides a user-friendly interface, code editor, and helpful features for data analysis. RStudio Desktop is free and works with base R.
Note: You must install base R first before installing RStudio.


R in Google Colab
Access R in Google Colab: R in Colab
Google Colab provides free access to R in a cloud-based notebook environment. This is useful if you don’t want to install R on your computer or need to work from different devices.


R in Jupyter Notebook
Install R for Jupyter: IRkernel Documentation
Jupyter Notebook is another notebook environment that supports R. To use R in Jupyter:

Install base R first
Install Jupyter Notebook or JupyterLab
Install the IRkernel package in R
Register the kernel with Jupyter

Note: R is also installed on all campus computers, so you can use the computer labs if you prefer not to install software on your personal computer.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Description (Catalog Description)
Descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions, confidence intervals, contingency tests, regression and correlation, tests of hypothesis, analysis of variance. Emphasis: methods and applications used in the biological and natural resource sciences.
Division: Lower Division  Weekly: Lecture 3 hrs. Activity 2 hrs.  Instruction Mode: In-person
Prerequisite: MATH 101 or MATH 101I or MATH 102 or MATH 109B (may be taken concurrently with instructor approval) with a grade of C- or better or ALEKS PPL assessment score over 65 or appropriate high school coursework. See Math Placement and ALEKS PPL for details. MATH 102 may be taken concurrently with instructor approval.
Note: if you plan to use STAT 109 as a prerequisite for another MATH, DATA or STAT course, you must have a grade of C- or better in this course.

Course Learning Outcomes
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

Use numerical and graphical descriptive statistics to summarize and interpret data.
Explain the role of randomness, probability, and sampling in statistical inference.
Apply probability rules and work with common probability distributions.
Construct and interpret confidence intervals for means and proportions.
Conduct and interpret basic hypothesis tests.
Analyze relationships between pairs of variables using correlation and regression.
Use a statistical programming language to analyze data and communicate results.
Distinguish between correlation and causation and recognize limitations of statistical conclusions.



General Education & Institutional Learning Outcomes
This course fulfills Lower Division General Education Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning) and supports Cal Poly Humboldt’s Institutional Learning Outcomes related to quantitative reasoning, critical thinking, and communication.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Late or Missed Work Policies
The policies for late or missed work are as follows:

Daily attendance in lecture is required, but I know that sometimes you will need to miss class due to illness or other emergencies. Each student may miss up to 10 days of lecture without penalty and without contacting me. It’s up to you to reserve these days for emergencies only. In my experience, it is rare for a student to succeed if they regularly miss lecture or are absent for more than two weeks so if you find yourself in this situation, please communicate with me and your advisor so we can help you find a solution to getting back on track.
Weekly quizzes during Thursday lab sessions are required, but I know that sometimes you will need to miss lab due to illness or other emergencies. If this happens, please contact me to make an arrangement to take the quiz at another time.
Weekly programming labs are due Tuesdays in Canvas. After the due date, a solution will be posted, so late submissions are generally not accepted except in documented emergencies. These labs will be graded on completion and your reflection.
Projects are due on the specified date in Canvas. You may submit a late project up to a week late with no penalty. After a week, a project can be turn in for 70% of the grade.
Midterms are required. If you need to miss a midterm due to illness or other emergencies, please communicate with me as soon as possible to reschedule it. Alternatively, you may take the optional final exam (Wednesday, May 13, 2026, 10:20–12:10 PM, BSS 166) to replace a missed midterm grade.

Note: Technology issues (internet access, device failure, software problems) are not automatically considered valid excuses for late or missed work; students are expected to plan accordingly.


AI Policy
Much of your course grade will be based on materials that you produce in-class without having AI as a resource. But you may use AI, such as NotebookLM or ChatGPT, in this course to help you learn the materials or to perform data analyses or on the projects.
For work that you do at home that is turned in for a grade I have two requirements:

if you used AI, acknowledge this briefly in the document (you don’t need to state in detail how you used AI or give your prompts, just the name of the AI service you used).

Be responsible for the correctness and appropriateness of all (AI assisted or not) materials you turn in.

I may use AI to assist me in my delivery of the course material, such as asking AI to see if an assignment is appropriate for a college level course or if the wording of a quiz question could be improved. I will not use AI to grade your work or use an AI detection tool to check your work.
